blocks.text: You yourselves are our letter, written on our hearts, to be known and read by all;
and you show that you are a letter of Christ, prepared by us, written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts.

2–3 : Paul defends his lack of such a letter since the Corinthian congregants constitute his . Contrasting tablets of stone (the medium for the Ten Commandments, e.g., Ex 20.1–17; 24.12; 31.18; 34.1) with human hearts, Paul invokes the “new covenant (see v. 6) language of Jer 31.31–33 and Ezek 11.19; 36.26–27.
Such is the confidence that we have through Christ toward God.
Not that we are competent of ourselves to claim anything as coming from us; our competence is from God,
who has made us competent to be ministers of a new covenant, not of letter but of spirit; for the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life.

4–6 : Paul's own reiterates the source of Paul's ministry. Concentrated “diakonos” (“servant, minister”) language in chs 1–9 supports Paul's claim to be an authoritative go-between. for the new covenant ministry is from God and bears the life of the Spirit in contrast to the dead letter of the written code.
Now if the ministry of death, chiseled in letters on stone tablets, came in glory so that the people of Israel could not gaze at Moses’ face because of the glory of his face, a glory now set aside,
how much more will the ministry of the Spirit come in glory?
For if there was glory in the ministry of condemnation, much more does the ministry of justification abound in glory!
Indeed, what once had glory has lost its glory because of the greater glory;
for if what was set aside came through glory, much more has the permanent come in glory!

7–11 : connect with Moses’ reflection of divine glory at Sinai (Ex 34.29–35). An extended three-part rhetorical figure, known in rabbinic literature as “qal va-ḥomer” (lit., “light and heavy,” i.e., “from the lesser to the greater”), follows through v. 11. Cf. Rom 5.16,18.
Since, then, we have such a hope, we act with great boldness,
not like Moses, who put a veil over his face to keep the people of Israel from gazing at the end of the glory that was being set aside.
13 : may suggest simply “cessation,” or alternately, “goal.”
But their minds were hardened. Indeed, to this very day, when they hear the reading of the old covenant, that same veil is still there, since only in Christ is it set aside.

14 : recalls Deut. 29.4; Isa 29.10 (cited verbatim in Rom 11.8). Divine agency may be reflected in the passive voice of the verb “to harden.” Temporal distinctions (cf. Deut 29.4 “but to this day”) between the time of Moses and the present collapse: the veil remains when they hear the read. A second passive verb completes the thought: only in Christ is the veil .
Indeed, to this very day whenever Moses is read, a veil lies over their minds;
15 : Paul reiterates the temporal duration “to this day.” Heart (Gk “kardia”; NRSV ) is now the veiled organ.
but when one turns to the Lord, the veil is removed.
16 : Partial human agency appears within the passive construction: the veil ; is indefinite.
Now the Lord is the Spirit, and where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is freedom.
17 : (Ex 34.34) is identified by Paul with the and the Spirit with (cf. v. 18; Gal 5.1–5).

And all of us, with unveiled faces, seeing the glory of the Lord as though reflected in a mirror, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another; for this comes from the Lord, the Spirit.
1.12–7.16: Body, Part I: Visitation, trauma, reconciliation.
2.14–3.18: Divine commissioning and ministry in the new covenant.
12–18 : Paul contrasts his own boldness to Moses’ hiding.

18 : continues Paul's emphasis on throughout the Corinthian correspondence. , cf. Moses’ repeated unveiling in Ex 34.34. may suggest the reflection of the in (4.6). Some translate the verb differently, e.g., “to behold,” “to contemplate.” Metamorphosis to the divine figures in Hellenistic religion and in Rom 8.29; 1 Cor 15.49; Col 3.10.
